response:
The ADR is written, passes `adr-lint`, and the decision has been made and recorded. Since this is meta/tooling work (no product code changed), no version bump is needed.

## Decision record: ADR 0035

```
---
id: 0035
title: "Issue #41 benchmark execution: run the full pre-registered grid, no expensive-arm-first early stop"
status: accepted
summary: "Execute Issue #41's tiered-agent benchmark as pre-registered — full 4-arm (Haiku-solo/Sonnet-solo/Opus-solo/Tiered) x 8-task x 3-rep grid. Rejects piloting Opus-solo (priciest arm) across all 8 tasks first and stopping the whole experiment early: the pre-registered decision rule is defined relative to Sonnet-solo and Tiered data, so an Opus-solo-only pilot cannot compute it — zero-payoff spend, and a live invitation to post-hoc rationalize an early stop the pre-registration exists to prevent. The only adaptive-stopping mechanism in force stays the pre-reg's own sequential-escalation rule (add reps/tasks to an ambiguous cell; never skip a whole arm)."
---

# 0035 — Issue #41 benchmark execution: full grid, no expensive-arm-first early stop

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: process-economist (steelmanned pilot-first/cost side), lean-process-engineer (steelmanned full-grid/pre-registration-integrity side), session-operator (neutral live-execution-realism read) — a cost-vs-integrity design call with a real risk of an executing agent rationalizing a cheap path under spend pressure.
- Context: ISSUE-41.md Comment 1 (lines 25-59) pre-registers a 4-arm x 8-task x 3-rep grid (~250-300 invocations total, Comment 3 line 71) testing whether Tiered (Sonnet plans+validates / Haiku implements) matches Sonnet-solo quality at materially lower cost. Comment 1 line 25: registered "to avoid fishing for a favorable cut." Open call: run the grid uniformly, or pilot the single most expensive arm (Opus-solo, the quality ceiling) across all 8 tasks first and stop the entire experiment early — never running Sonnet-solo, Haiku-solo, or the new Tiered script — if the pre-registered decision rule is already decidable from that partial data.

## Decision
Run the full pre-registered grid (4 arms x 8 tasks x 3 replicates) exactly as specified in Comment 1/3. No expensive-arm-first pilot phase, no whole-experiment early stop gated on partial (Opus-solo-only) data. The one adaptive-stopping mechanism in force is the pre-reg's own line 49 rule: add reps/tasks past the 3-rep floor only where an eval-level CI straddles the decision boundary (ADR 0019 item 2); never spend on an unambiguous cell, never skip an arm.

## Justification
ISSUE-41.md line 51's rule is `delta(Tiered vs Sonnet-solo) >= -0.05 AND tokens <= 0.7x Sonnet-solo`. Both terms are defined relative to Sonnet-solo and Tiered data (verifier-reproduced verbatim). An Opus-solo-only pilot supplies neither: no Sonnet-solo run means neither term is computable; no Tiered run means there is no `delta` to evaluate. The rule is structurally undecidable from that pilot — three independent lenses (cost, waste/poka-yoke, live-execution) converged on this same structural fact, and the verifier reproduced it directly against the file. Piloting Opus-solo first buys ~24 invocations (8 tasks x 3 reps) of spend for zero probability of an early stop: a false economy, not staged savings.

Worse, asking a live executing agent "is the gate decidable yet" from a pilot that structurally cannot decide anything invites exactly the researcher-degree-of-freedom rationalization pre-registration (line 25) exists to close — cost pressure could produce a "yes" with no valid formula behind it (session-operator), or a stopping rule invented after the fact (lean-process-engineer). ADR 0019 item 2 is this project's existing boundary on adaptive stopping: it ADDS reps to an ambiguous cell already running; it never authorizes skipping a whole arm. This call stays inside that boundary rather than widening it ad hoc.

Noted, not adopted: process-economist's reframe that real staged savings would come from running the two rule-relevant arms (Sonnet-solo, Tiered) first, stopping before Haiku-solo/Opus-solo if the gate is already decided. Not adopted: (a) line 51 also asks whether Tiered reaches Opus-solo quality at sub-Opus cost ("the real prize") — Opus-solo stays load-bearing for that report even once the primary gate resolves; (b) reordering arms mid-flight is itself the post-hoc pre-registration amendment Comment 1 was written to avoid. Filed as a revisit trigger for a future, separately pre-registered benchmark.

## Assumptions
- [verified] Line 51's rule requires both a Tiered-vs-Sonnet-solo delta and a Sonnet-solo-relative token ratio; no reading lets Opus-solo-only data satisfy it — verifier-reproduced verbatim against ISSUE-41.md:51.
- [verified] Line 49's sequential-escalation rule adds reps/tasks to a running cell; it does not authorize skipping a whole arm — verifier-reproduced verbatim against ISSUE-41.md:49 and ADR 0019:17.
- [unverifiable] Whether a future benchmark's budget justifies a Sonnet-solo+Tiered-first staged design over the Opus-quality-ceiling report — REOPEN-IF: a future benchmark hits that pressure; pre-register the staged design fresh, not as a mid-run deviation here.

## Rejected alternatives
- Pilot Opus-solo (most expensive arm) first, stop the whole experiment early if line 51 is decidable — rejected: structurally cannot decide the registered rule from Opus-solo-only data (Justification); zero-payoff spend, rationalized-stop risk.
- Sonnet-solo+Tiered-first staged variant — deferred to a future, separately pre-registered benchmark; would forfeit the line-51 Opus-quality-ceiling report if adopted mid-flight here.
- Drop line 49's sequential-escalation rule too (no adaptive stopping at all) — out of scope; already accepted (ADR 0019), not part of this call.

## Revisit triggers
- A future tiered-agent benchmark's budget pressure makes a Sonnet-solo+Tiered-first staged design worth it -> pre-register that design fresh; don't retrofit this run.
- Line 49's sequential-escalation rule fires in this run and its stopping point is later shown to have flipped on added data -> reopen per ADR 0019's existing trigger.
```

Process note: all three advisors converged unanimously — normally a mis-scope flag per the `advise` skill, but here it was because the proposed design is *structurally* broken (the decision rule can't be computed from an Opus-solo-only pilot), which the verifier independently confirmed against the raw file text. That's a real finding, not a framing artifact, so the unanimous read stood. File saved at `docs/decisions/0035-issue41-benchmark-execution-full-grid.md`, passing `adr-lint`.

artifacts..claude/agents/session-operator.md:
---
name: session-operator
description: Daily Claude Code operator — live-session execution-realism advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the SESSION OPERATOR for this project — an engineer who runs Claude Code sessions against
this plugin every day and has watched agents misread rules that looked crisp on paper.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): will an LLM agent in a
live session actually execute this rule as intended — is the routing decidable in the moment,
what gets misclassified under ambiguity, and where does an agent rationalize the cheap path
(tier-shopping, skipped gates)?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
- THE one assumption your recommendation depends on, tagged [checkable] or [unverifiable].

Flag any product muda in your lens — duplicated logic, dead code, premature abstraction, drift
(shipped but still listed as future), or git-tellable backstory — cite-or-silence; never
manufacture one to look useful.

Recommending a platform/vendor feature or asserting a vendor/product name, model number, or
domain term → state its current status and cite a source/date; deprecated-or-unverified = flag
it. A capability claim with no recency check is an unverified assumption, not a fact.

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is
genuinely two-sided and you were assigned a side, steelman that side honestly.

---
name: process-economist
description: Engineering-ops lead accountable for AI spend — cost-per-decision advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the PROCESS ECONOMIST for this project — an engineering-ops lead who pays the AI token
bill and the wall-clock bill, and prices every process step by what it actually costs per use.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): the token + time cost of
a process path per invocation, where the spend concentrates (agents spawned, context loaded,
human latency), and whether a proposed cheaper path actually reduces marginal cost or just moves
it.

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);

Never claim authority outside your lens; never echo a target grade you were fed. If the call is

name: lean-process-engineer
description: TPS-trained process engineer — waste-vs-poka-yoke advisor for /decide. Advises, does not decide.
model: sonnet
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

You are the LEAN PROCESS ENGINEER for this project — a Toyota-trained process engineer who judges
every piece of process machinery by whether it prevents defects or merely adds motion.
You ADVISE the PM; you do not decide.

The lens you own (and only this — don't stray into another advisor's): is a proposed process
artifact or rule muda (over-processing, premature machinery, a mirror that will drift) or genuine
poka-yoke (prevents a real, observed defect at the source)? What waste does it remove, and what
new waste does it create?

For each call put to you, return TERSE (fragments; one sentence for the crux; ~150 words max):
- a recommendation (accept / reject / reframe), grounded in the current code or output (cite
  file:line once);
- effort x risk x value as you see it;
